speciesProfiles.uncertaintyNote: Florida flowering behavior is documented better than pollination biology; keep the fertility default conservative.
Ambarella seasonality is clearer than its backyard fertility behavior, so the app should keep the pollination default conservative.
American persimmons are often functionally dioecious or variably self-fruitful, so a separate species lane is safer than the old generic persimmon bucket.
Most apples still need a second compatible cultivar or crabapple pollinizer, even though a few backyard standards are partially self-fruitful.
Apricots bloom very early and are frost-sensitive, so even reviewed cultivar timing should be treated as seasonal guidance rather than a hard date promise.
Asian pears are usually best planted with two or more compatible cultivars, while partially self-fruitful exceptions like Hosui still crop better with overlap.
Natural set can be weak. Hand pollination or strong beetle activity often improves crops.
Avocado fruit set depends heavily on temperature-distorted A/B flowering overlap. Many cultivars can fruit alone, but opposite-type companions usually improve reliability.
Edible bananas are best treated as continuously active rather than date-precise bloomers, because flowering depends more on plant age and heat than a stable annual window.
Acerola can flower in repeated warm-season flushes, so the model is more useful for watch windows than exact dates.
Fruiting black mulberries are generally treated as self-fruitful backyard trees, but the app should still keep the bloom window broad.
Black raspberries are generally self-fruitful, but they bloom and manage differently enough from red raspberries that they should keep their own lane.
Black sapote fertility and flowering behavior vary by cultivar, so both pollination guidance and timing should stay conservative.
Most blackberries are self-fruitful, but cane habit and primocane-fruiting selections still matter more than the coarse species window suggests.
Rabbiteye and highbush bloom overlap is not uniform, so cross-pollination guidance is stronger than the generic species timing.
Some cultivars can set alone, while others benefit from or require cross-pollination.
Calamondin often reblooms in mild climates, but a broad spring-centered baseline is still better than burying it under citrus.
Canary melon is another annual dessert melon where sowing date and maturity class matter more than a perennial orchard baseline, so auto bloom should remain suppressed.
Canistel bloom timing is seasonally useful, but set and flowering intensity still vary enough that the baseline should stay coarse.
Cantaloupe bloom timing is driven by sowing date and season length more than a perennial climate baseline, so the app should not pretend to know exact bloom windows automatically.
Casaba is a late annual winter-melon type, so flowering depends far more on sowing date than on orchard climate; suppression is the honest default.
Cashew bloom timing is usable as a seasonal cue, but cultivar fertility behavior is mixed enough that the app should keep both pollination and countdown precision conservative.
Cherimoya often benefits from hand pollination or strong beetle activity, so a separate lane is more honest than folding it into atemoya.
Citron is uncommon but distinct enough in home collections that it should not be collapsed into lemon.
Useful as a generic fallback, but citrus bloom behavior diverges by type; lemons and limes rebloom more than sweet orange, mandarin, or grapefruit.

Coconut flowering is effectively continuous in warm climates and fertility varies by dwarf, tall, and hybrid types, so exact timing and a single species-wide pollination rule would both be misleading.
The app models the common Arabica-type home-coffee lane here; bloom often comes in rain-triggered flushes, so local moisture patterns should outrank the generic warm-season watch window.
Common figs do not require caprification and are the standard backyard fig type.
Cranberries are generally self-fruitful, but bee activity and cross-pollination often improve set enough that the species lane should not imply complete pollination independence.
Most currants set alone, but mixed plantings still help stabilize yield and are common in home orchards.
Pollination coverage is strong, but cultivar bloom timing still varies by climate and repeat-wave behavior; local history should outrank the catalog window.
Elderberries often crop better with another compatible cultivar nearby, so the baseline should favor cross-pollination.
European pears often benefit from or require a second compatible cultivar, even though partially self-fruitful exceptions like Bartlett and Anjou exist.
European plums usually bloom later than Japanese plums and are more often self-fruitful, but nearby pollinizers still improve consistency.
Pineapple guavas often crop better with another cultivar nearby even when a single variety can set some fruit.
Most home gardeners mean common figs when they say fig. Smyrna and San Pedro types behave differently and need their own lanes.
Galia is a muskmelon-type annual crop; bloom timing follows planting date and heat accumulation, so the app should avoid fake automatic bloom windows.
Gooseberries are usually self-fruitful, so the species model can stay simple unless cultivar-specific data proves otherwise.
The baseline mostly fits bunch grapes, while muscadine and wild-grape fertility behavior can differ enough that the species row stays intentionally broad.
Grapefruit and pummelo bloom is mostly spring-centered, but hybrid lines can drift earlier or later than the baseline.
Green sapote is less consistently documented than white or mamey sapote, so the catalog season should be treated as a coarse baseline.
Common guava often flowers in repeated warm-season flushes rather than a single bloom burst, so the forecast should act more like an orchard-aware active season than one exact annual window.
Hardy hybrid plums are self-incompatible and need a compatible second variety; they should not be folded into the Japanese or European plum lanes.
Haskaps need a compatible second cultivar for reliable crops, so the species default should stay cross-pollinated.
Honeydew behaves like other annual melons: flowering depends mostly on planting date and maturity, so auto bloom forecasting should stay suppressed.
Jaboticaba is better modeled as a repeat bloomer than a single seasonal tree, but species-level fertility guidance is still too thin for a stronger claim.
Warm-climate bloom can stretch in multiple flushes, and pollination biology is documented less cleanly than the cultivar list.
Jamaican cherry can flower almost continuously in warm climates, so exact countdowns would be fake precision.
Oriental persimmons are usually more self-fruitful than American types, so they deserve their own species defaults.
Japanese plums usually bloom earlier than European plums and many need or strongly benefit from a compatible pollinizer.
Most fuzzy kiwifruit cultivars need a compatible pollinizer, and bloom overlap matters more than the broad seasonal window.
Most kiwiberry vines need a compatible male pollinizer, while partially self-fertile exceptions like Issai still crop better when cross-pollinated.
Kumquats are usually self-fruitful and spring-centered, so a separate species row is more useful than hiding them under generic citrus.
Many lemons flower more than once each year, especially in mild climates, so the app should treat lemon as a repeat-bearing citrus rather than a single spring-only bloomer.
Limes often rebloom in warm climates and some types can flower in nearly any month, so the app should model lime as a repeat-bearing citrus, not an annual-only bloom.
Longan flowering depends heavily on cool, dry-season conditions, so the species baseline should stay seasonal but not overly precise.
Loquat is often self-fruitful, but several named cultivars still crop better with cross-pollination, so the species lane should stay conservative.
Bloom induction depends heavily on local cool-season conditions, and cultivar fruit set can vary even when flowering looks strong.
Mamey sapote bloom and fruiting cycles are long and climate-sensitive, so local observation history should outrank the generic season.
Often treated as functionally dioecious in backyard use; avoid assuming a lone tree will crop well.
Mandarin, tangelo, and tangor bloom overlap varies by hybrid background, so cultivar phase is more trustworthy than one shared species window.
Mango usually blooms in one main annual flush, but bloom strength and set still move with weather, pollinator activity, and local cultivar behavior.
Fruiting mulberries are often self-fruitful in home gardens, but white mulberry also includes many fruitless male trees and some trees carry separate sexes.
Muscadines need their own lane because female and self-fertile cultivars do not share one pollination rule.
Most nectarines are self-fruitful, but chill fulfillment and frost exposure can move the real bloom window more than the catalog baseline suggests.
Northern highbush cultivars are often self-fruitful, but cross-pollination still improves berry size and earliness.
Many olives will set with a single cultivar, but mixed plantings are still a safer default because bloom overlap and compatibility vary by type.
Sweet oranges usually have one main spring bloom, but local climate and stress can still create lighter off-cycle flowering.
Sex expression and planting type drive papaya fruit set more than cultivar name alone, so the species model should stay coarse and history-led.
Purple, yellow, and hybrid passionfruit lines do not share one fertility rule, and pruning plus climate can shift repeat flush timing.
Most pawpaws crop better with a genetically different tree nearby and often depend on beetle or fly pollination, so the species lane should assume cross-pollination.
Peach bloom is strongly affected by chill completion and frost timing, so the catalog window is best treated as a seasonal expectation, not a guarantee.
European pears, Asian pears, and hybrid pears do not all share one pollination rule, so the generic pear lane should stay a cautious fallback.
Pecans need a compatible partner because protandrous and protogynous cultivars do not self-cover reliably.
Persian melons are annual muskmelon types with bloom tied to planting timing and heat, so orchard-style automatic bloom forecasts should stay suppressed.
American and Oriental persimmons do not share one fertility rule, so the generic persimmon row should stay cautious about both timing and pollination.
Pineapple flowering can be induced and staggered by stress or management, so the catalog window is only a broad seasonal cue.
Japanese, European, and hardy hybrid plums do not share identical bloom timing or fertility behavior, so the generic plum lane should stay a cautious fallback.
Pomegranates are usually self-fruitful, but climate, heat, and insect activity still affect set quality enough that the species bloom window should stay approximate.
Pummelos are close to grapefruit seasonally, but they deserve a separate catalog lane instead of disappearing into the generic grapefruit bucket.
Rabbiteyes need a second compatible rabbiteye cultivar for reliable crops, so they should not share one fertility rule with highbush blueberries.
Most raspberries are self-fruitful, but primocane versus floricane production and black-raspberry timing differences make the generic species lane intentionally broad.
Red raspberries are usually self-fruitful, but home growers still need cultivar-aware cane-type expectations because summer-bearing and primocane selections do not fruit the same way.
Cultivated sugar cane is usually grown vegetatively and flowering is often suppressed, so bloom forecasting is intentionally de-emphasized here.
San Pedro figs can set a breba crop without pollination, but the main crop depends on caprification, so the species lane should warn about pollination.
Sapodilla can flower across a long warm season or nearly continuously in the tropics, so the model should behave like a repeat-bloom climate season instead of one short spring window.
Serviceberries often set alone, but cross-pollination can still improve fruit size and consistency in mixed plantings.
Smyrna figs need pollination from caprifigs for the edible crop, so they should not share the common-fig fertility rule.
Sour cherries are commonly self-fruitful, but spring weather and nearby pollinizers can still shift the real crop quality away from the nominal bloom lane.
Soursop flowering is warm-season driven, but pollination detail and flush strength still vary enough that the generic window should stay conservative.
Southern highbush can set with self-pollination, but mixed plantings improve fruit size, timing, and consistency.
Star fruit often flowers in repeated flushes, but cultivar fertility and flush strength vary enough that local observations should outrank the baseline.
Cultivated strawberries are self-fertile, but pollinator activity still affects berry fullness enough that the seasonal window should stay coarse.
Natural set can be weak and hand pollination often changes outcomes, so the species window is more useful as a watch period than a precise forecast.
Most sweet cherries need pollinizers, but self-fertile exceptions and regional chill differences make the generic bloom lane intentionally conservative.
Pollination guidance is stronger than the exact bloom calendar here; warm-season timing can still drift by climate and tree vigor.
Watermelon bloom timing depends heavily on planting date and maturity class, so automatic orchard-style season forecasts would be fake precision.
White mulberry can carry male and female flowers on separate trees or on the same tree, so fruiting selections are best treated as usually self-fruitful but not perfectly uniform.
Pollination is cultivar-dependent and sources conflict, so the long bloom season should be treated as a coarse watch window.
Yuzu behaves more like a cool-season acid citrus than a lemon or lime, so keeping it distinct avoids the wrong cultivar lane.
